description: MetalLB L2 Mode — controller pool metrics + speaker ARP/NDP health, VIP-to-node ownership, failover severity ranking. Scaled for large (15+ node) clusters.

panels.title: 🔵  Cluster Status — Config, Pool & Speaker Health

panels.description: Aggregate count of speakers with config loaded. Must equal total speaker count (= node count in L2 mode). If a broken YAML config is applied, this drops below total — speakers stop updating routing tables.
panels.title: Speakers Config Loaded (sum)

panels.description: Aggregate count of speakers running stale config. Must be 0. Any value >0 means at least one speaker failed to reload the latest L2Advertisement/IPAddressPool — investigate that node immediately.
panels.title: Speakers Config Stale (sum)

panels.description: Total IPs in use vs total available. From metallb-controller (allocator metrics).
panels.title: IPs In Use

panels.description: Total usable IPs across all pools (controller allocator).
panels.title: Total Pool IPs

panels.description: Free IPs = total - in_use. Near zero = pool exhaustion risk.
panels.title: Free IPs Remaining
🟣  Node Leadership Balance — L2 VIP Distribution

panels.description: Count of VIPs/services actively owned (announced) per node. In a healthy, balanced L2 cluster these bars should be roughly even. A single node with a disproportionately long bar is hogging L2 leadership — a scaling and blast-radius risk, since that node's failure would trigger a mass failover.
panels.title: Active Services Per Node — L2 Leadership Distribution

panels.description: Live mapping of which Node and Speaker Pod currently announces which VIP, for which Service. Trimmed to operationally relevant labels only (container/endpoint/job/namespace/prometheus instance-scrape labels dropped). This answers: 'which VIP is this node serving and for which service'.
panels.title: Node → Speaker Pod → VIP → Service (live)
📡  L2 ARP/NDP — Requests, Responses & Drop Detection (per VIP)

panels.description:
Tracks per-second rate of incoming Layer 2 discovery broadcasts (ARP requests for IPv4 / NDP requests for IPv6) targeting MetalLB-managed LoadBalancer IPs.

What it means:
• Spikes: new client machines joining the network, expired router ARP caches, or potential network loops/storms.
• Baseline: healthy, quiet networks show very low fractional decimal values (m req/s = milli-requests/sec), meaning external clients have already cached the node's MAC address and rarely re-query.

Healthy target: sustained baseline under ~100 m req/s (0.1 req/s) per VIP. Sustained values above 1 req/s per VIP warrant investigation for ARP storms or flapping upstream switches.

Metric: metallb_layer2_requests_received
panels.title: ARP/NDP Requests Received (per VIP)

panels.description:
Tracks per-second rate of outgoing Layer 2 answers (ARP replies for IPv4 / NDP neighbor advertisements for IPv6) sent by MetalLB.

What it means:
• Healthy state: this metric should exactly match Requests Received. A 1-to-1 ratio proves the cluster is acknowledging all network queries.
• Drops/discrepancies: if this value drops below the request rate, the speaker pod is failing to answer network queries — this WILL break external ingress traffic for that VIP.

Metric: metallb_layer2_responses_sent
panels.title: ARP/NDP Responses Sent (per VIP)

panels.description:
Cluster-wide delta: total requests received minus total responses sent, per second. In a perfectly healthy network this line sits flat at zero.

If this value rises above 0 for a sustained period, MetalLB is ignoring network clients — external ingress is being silently dropped for at least one VIP.

Suggested alert: trigger if value > 0 for more than 2 minutes.

Formula: sum(rate(metallb_layer2_requests_received[$__rate_interval])) - sum(rate(metallb_layer2_responses_sent[$__rate_interval]))
panels.title: MetalLB L2 Dropped/Ignored Requests (cluster-wide) — Must stay at 0

panels.description:
Tracks per-second rate of unsolicited Layer 2 network updates (Gratuitous ARP for IPv4 / unsolicited NDP advertisement for IPv6) sent by MetalLB, per VIP.

What it means:
• Baseline (m pps): small fractional values = standard background tick keeping physical switch MAC tables fresh.
• Spikes: heavy spikes = failover or cluster update, where a service migrates to a new leader node and forces the network to redirect traffic.

Real-world scenarios:
• Normal ops: steady ~12 mp/s — cluster stable, just reminder packets.
• Active failover: sharp spike to 500+ mp/s then drops back to baseline — node crashed/maintained, new leader elected, switch flooded once to redirect traffic without dropping connections.
• Flapping (critical): continuous erratic mountain-shaped spikes that never return to baseline — split-brain, two+ nodes fighting over the same VIP. Severe internal cluster networking issue.

Metric: metallb_layer2_gratuitous_sent
panels.title: Gratuitous ARP/NDP Rate (per VIP)

panels.description:
Tracks per-second rate of gratuitous ARP/NDP sent by MetalLB, mapped directly to the hosting physical OpenShift Node (joins metallb_layer2_gratuitous_sent with kube_pod_info on pod label).

What it emphasizes:
• Node leadership: which physical server is actively managing traffic redirection / routing table freshness for your LoadBalancer IPs.
• Failover dynamics: visualizes traffic migration between host machines during node reboots or cluster upgrades.

Troubleshooting scenarios:
• Stable: lines hover flat 0.01–0.1 pps — healthy, just keeping switch MAC tables fresh.
• Standard pod update / micro-failover: one node briefly spikes to ~1 pps then drops back — app pod relocated, MetalLB updated switch seamlessly.
• Complete node outage: one node's line drops to 0 pps while another simultaneously spikes 10+ pps — failed node, successful failover to survivor, which blasted the network to pull traffic.
• Split-brain/flapping (critical): multiple node lines jumping up/down simultaneously in a chaotic zig-zag — two nodes competing for the same VIP, severe packet drops for external users.

Requires kube-state-metrics (kube_pod_info) scraped in the same Prometheus.

Metric: metallb_layer2_gratuitous_sent joined with kube_pod_info{namespace="metallb-system"}
panels.title: Gratuitous ARP/NDP Rate (per Node) — Leadership & Failover Mapping

panels.description:
FIXED v2: gated to avoid divide-by-near-zero blowup. Old formula (100 * grat_rate / (req_rate + 0.001)) produced fake values like 133,000% whenever request_rate was near-zero — pure math artifact, not real instability, because most L2 VIPs naturally get near-zero ARP traffic once clients cache the MAC.

This version only computes the ratio when request_rate is non-trivial (> 0.01 req/s, i.e. real client traffic present). When traffic is below that floor, the panel returns no data instead of a misleading huge percentage — gaps in the line are expected and mean 'too quiet to judge', not an error.

How to read it now:
• No line / gap: VIP is quiet, normal for L2 — ratio is not meaningful here, ignore.
• Line present, <50%: healthy — gratuitous sends are a small fraction of real traffic.
• Line present, >50% sustained: real instability — leader is re-announcing far more than client demand justifies. Investigate pod crash-looping or split-brain on that VIP.

For raw instability regardless of traffic level, trust the 'Total Gratuitous Sends Ranked' panel below instead — that one is not affected by this math issue.

Formula: (req_rate > 0.01) ? 100 * grat_rate / req_rate : no data
panels.title: Gratuitous / Requests Ratio % (traffic-gated) — gaps = too quiet to judge, line >50% = real instability
🔴  Failover Severity — Cumulative Gratuitous Sends Ranked

panels.description:
Descending rank of raw, cumulative gratuitous packets sent by each MetalLB speaker/VIP. Immediately exposes which VIP or host node is generating the highest volume of unsolicited network table updates.

How to interpret values over 25,000 (25k):
• Healthy (slow accumulation): if 25k took weeks of continuous uptime to reach, it's normal background maintenance behavior.
• Critical (rapid vertical spike): if the counter reaches 25k within minutes or hours, a critical network failure is occurring.

Common causes of rapid 25k+ spikes:
1. Micro-failover storms — an app pod crash-looping, forcing MetalLB to migrate the VIP back and forth endlessly.
2. Split-brain routing — a physical network partition causing multiple nodes to believe they're the active leader simultaneously, fighting for control of the IP.

Formula: sort_desc(metallb_layer2_gratuitous_sent)
panels.title: Total Gratuitous Sends Ranked (per VIP) — bar length = failover severity

title: MetalLB — L2 Mode
